questions.stem: A new Snowflake account is provisioned. Which role is automatically granted to every user created in the account, including service accounts?

options.text: SYSADMIN
USERADMIN
PUBLIC
SECURITYADMIN

questions.explanation: The PUBLIC role is automatically granted to every user in a Snowflake account. It represents the lowest common denominator of access. Privileges granted to PUBLIC are available to all users. SYSADMIN, USERADMIN, and SECURITYADMIN are system roles that must be explicitly granted to users — they are not automatic.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to enforce that all new users must change their password on first login. Which parameter in the CREATE USER statement controls this?

options.text: FORCE_PASSWORD_RESET = TRUE
MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = TRUE
PASSWORD_EXPIRY = IMMEDIATE
CHANGE_ON_LOGIN = TRUE

questions.explanation: MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = TRUE forces the user to change their password at the next login. This is a best practice for new user accounts. The other options (FORCE_PASSWORD_RESET, PASSWORD_EXPIRY = IMMEDIATE, CHANGE_ON_LOGIN) are not valid Snowflake user parameters.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is designing a role hierarchy for a healthcare organization. Select TWO best practices that should be followed when designing this hierarchy.

options.text: Grant custom roles to SYSADMIN so SYSADMIN can manage objects owned by those roles
Use ACCOUNTADMIN as the daily operational role for all senior DBAs to ensure maximum privileges
Create separate functional roles (e.g., ANALYST, ENGINEER) and access roles (e.g., READ_DB1, WRITE_DB1), granting access roles to functional roles
Grant all custom roles directly to PUBLIC to ensure all users have access to common resources

questions.explanation: Granting custom roles to SYSADMIN ensures SYSADMIN can manage all objects through the inherited role hierarchy — critical for centralized administration. Separating functional roles from access roles is a Snowflake best practice: functional roles represent job functions and are granted to users; access roles represent specific object permissions and are granted to functional roles. This separation makes auditing and privilege management cleaner. Using ACCOUNTADMIN for daily operations violates least privilege. Granting all roles to PUBLIC gives every user those privileges, which is a major security violation.
questions.reference: Role Design: Functional vs Access Roles

questions.stem: A Snowflake account administrator sets the following at the account level: ALTER ACCOUNT SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. A developer then creates a table with CREATE TABLE orders (...). What is the Time Travel retention period for the orders table?

options.text: 0 days, because new tables default to no retention
1 day, which is the Snowflake default for Standard edition
14 days, because the table inherits the account-level setting
90 days, which is the maximum allowed retention period

questions.explanation: When DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS is set at the account level and no explicit value is set at the database, schema, or table level, the object inherits the account-level setting. Since the table was created without specifying a retention period, it inherits the account setting of 14 days. Note that 14 days of Time Travel requires Enterprise edition or higher — Standard edition is limited to 1 day. The question implies Enterprise edition context.

questions.stem: A company is implementing Snowflake in a regulated environment and needs to ensure that the SECURITYADMIN role cannot be used to access business data, only to manage security. Which architectural control achieves this?

options.text: Remove the SECURITYADMIN role from all users and only assign it when needed via a PAM tool
Ensure that no data access roles (SELECT, INSERT, etc.) are granted to SECURITYADMIN, and that SECURITYADMIN is not granted SYSADMIN or any data-owning custom role
Use a network policy to prevent SECURITYADMIN users from accessing data tables
Set STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0 for SECURITYADMIN to prevent data queries

questions.explanation: SECURITYADMIN inherits USERADMIN but does not inherently have data access. The risk comes from SECURITYADMIN's ability to GRANT any privilege to any role — including granting itself data access. The architectural control is to ensure no data privileges are granted to SECURITYADMIN and SECURITYADMIN is not placed in a role hierarchy that includes data-owning roles. Separating security administration from data access is a standard compliance control. Option A (PAM tool) is an operational control but not the architectural role design answer. Network policies cannot scope access by role. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT = 0 disables timeouts, not data access.
questions.reference: Account Administration: Separating Security from Data Access

questions.stem: An organization uses Snowflake's object hierarchy: Organization → Account → Database → Schema → Table. A login policy is created and needs to be assigned to a specific set of users. Which command correctly assigns a login policy to a user?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;
ALTER USER john_doe SET LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;
GRANT LOGIN_POLICY strict_login_policy TO USER john_doe;
CREATE USER john_doe WITH LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;

questions.explanation: Login policies are assigned to users using ALTER USER ... SET LOGIN_POLICY. A login policy can also be set at the account level (ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOGIN_POLICY) to apply to all users as a default, but assigning to a specific user requires the ALTER USER syntax. GRANT LOGIN_POLICY is not valid syntax — login policies are assigned, not granted. While CREATE USER can include many parameters, SET LOGIN_POLICY is not a CREATE USER parameter in standard Snowflake syntax — it must be applied with ALTER USER after creation.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to enumerate all users who have not logged in for more than 90 days. Select TWO approaches that can accomplish this.

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS and filter where LAST_SUCCESS_LOGIN < DATEADD('day', -90, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
Run SHOW USERS and check the last_success_login column
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY and find users with no records in the last 90 days
Run SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USER_HISTORY()) WHERE last_login < DATEADD('day', -90, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS view contains user metadata including LAST_SUCCESS_LOGIN, which can be queried with a date filter to find inactive users. SHOW USERS also surfaces last_success_login for each user. Both approaches work. ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY contains login attempt records and could be used indirectly (find users not present in recent 90 days), but it does not directly show 'last login' for all users — users who never logged in would be missed. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USER_HISTORY() is not a valid function in Snowflake.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake system role has the ability to manage grants (GRANT/REVOKE privileges) for ALL securable objects in the account, including granting any privilege to any role?

options.text: SYSADMIN
USERADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
PUBLIC

questions.explanation: SECURITYADMIN has the MANAGE GRANTS global privilege, which allows it to grant or revoke any privilege on any securable object in the account. This makes it the role responsible for all access control management. SYSADMIN creates and owns objects but does not have the broad MANAGE GRANTS privilege. USERADMIN can create users and roles but cannot grant arbitrary privileges on objects. PUBLIC has minimal privileges.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to see the current credit quota and the amount of credits already consumed for all resource monitors in the account. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY;
SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS;
DESCRIBE RESOURCE MONITOR all;

questions.explanation: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS displays all resource monitors with their quota, used credits, status, frequency, and trigger configuration. It is the direct DDL command for viewing resource monitor state. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows overall credit consumption by service type, not resource monitor details. There is no ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS view in Snowflake. DESCRIBE RESOURCE MONITOR all is not valid syntax.

questions.stem: A company wants to set up credit spend controls per department. The Data Engineering department has three warehouses (ETL_WH, STAGING_WH, TRANSFORM_WH) and a monthly budget of 2000 credits. How should the administrator configure resource monitoring?

options.text: Create one resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 2000, assign all three warehouses to it
Create three resource monitors each with CREDIT_QUOTA = 666, one per warehouse
Set CREDIT_QUOTA = 2000 at the account level and rely on overall account monitoring
Create a BUDGET object with BUDGET_LIMIT = 2000 assigned to the department role

questions.explanation: A single resource monitor can be assigned to multiple warehouses. Credits consumed by all assigned warehouses count toward the single shared quota. This is the correct approach for department-level spend control — one monitor with the total budget, assigned to all departmental warehouses. Splitting into three monitors (option B) would give each warehouse only 666 credits and they would not share the pool. Account-level monitoring (option C) does not isolate departmental spend. BUDGET is a valid Snowflake feature but BUDGET_LIMIT assigned to a role is not the correct BUDGET syntax — Budgets are assigned to specific objects or account-level.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is investigating why a warehouse was unexpectedly suspended mid-day. Select TWO queries that would help determine if a resource monitor triggered the suspension.

options.text: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS; — check STATUS and USED_CREDITS vs CREDIT_QUOTA
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE WAREHOUSE_NAME = 'MY_WH' AND ERROR_CODE IS NOT NULL ORDER BY START_TIME DESC;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY(WAREHOUSE_NAME => 'MY_WH')) WHERE EVENT_STATE = 'SUSPENDED';
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'WAREHOUSE_METERING' ORDER BY USAGE_DATE DESC;

questions.explanation: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS shows whether a monitor associated with the warehouse has STATUS = SUSPENDED and whether USED_CREDITS has reached or exceeded CREDIT_QUOTA. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY (an INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function) records warehouse lifecycle events including suspension events and their reasons — this can directly show if a resource monitor caused the suspension. QUERY_HISTORY with error codes shows failed queries but does not explain why the warehouse was suspended. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows credit usage totals, not suspension events.

questions.stem: A resource monitor is set with FREQUENCY = DAILY and CREDIT_QUOTA = 50. It is now 3 PM and 48 credits have been consumed. The SUSPEND trigger is set at 100%. A new large query is submitted that will consume approximately 5 credits. What happens?

options.text: The query is rejected before starting because submitting it would exceed the daily quota
The query starts and runs; when cumulative consumption hits 50 credits (100%), the warehouse is suspended after the query completes
The query starts and runs; when cumulative consumption hits 50 credits (100%), the warehouse suspends immediately even if the query is still running
The query is queued until midnight when the daily quota resets

questions.explanation: With a SUSPEND trigger (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY), the warehouse allows the currently running query to complete before suspending. The resource monitor detects that the 100% threshold is crossed while the query is running and queues the SUSPEND action, but the query finishes first. Only SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY would cancel the in-flight query. Snowflake does not pre-emptively reject queries based on predicted future consumption. Queries are not queued for the reset period.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: SUSPEND vs SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY Behavior

questions.stem: A company wants more granular credit monitoring than resource monitors provide — specifically, they want to track and alert on spending for individual Snowflake objects like a specific table with automatic clustering enabled. Which Snowflake feature should the administrator use?

options.text: Create a resource monitor with OBJECT_SCOPE = TABLE and assign it to the specific table
Use the BUDGET feature (CREATE BUDGET) to create a budget scoped to the specific object
Query METERING_DAILY_HISTORY daily with a custom alert threshold in an external monitoring tool
Enable CREDIT_TRACKING = TRUE on the table using ALTER TABLE

questions.explanation: The BUDGET feature (introduced after resource monitors) provides more granular spending control. Budgets can be scoped to individual Snowflake objects, specific accounts, or even specific services, allowing per-object credit tracking and alerting. Resource monitors cannot be scoped to individual objects like tables — they only apply at the warehouse level or account level. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY can provide data for custom monitoring but is not a built-in alerting feature. CREDIT_TRACKING = TRUE on ALTER TABLE is not a valid Snowflake parameter.

questions.stem: A developer reports getting the error 'Object does not exist or operation cannot be performed' when querying a table they believe they have access to. The administrator confirms the developer's role has SELECT on the table. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The developer needs the REFERENCES privilege in addition to SELECT
The developer's role is missing USAGE privilege on the database or schema containing the table
The table has been encrypted and the developer needs a decryption key
The developer needs to run USE WAREHOUSE before querying

questions.explanation: In Snowflake, to access an object a role must have USAGE on every container in the hierarchy: USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and then SELECT on the table. 'Object does not exist or operation cannot be performed' is Snowflake's generic error that appears when the role cannot even see the object — which happens when USAGE on the database or schema is missing. The object appears to not exist from the role's perspective. REFERENCES is for foreign key definitions, not needed for SELECT. Tables are not individually encrypted with customer keys in standard setups. A missing warehouse only prevents query execution with a different error.
questions.reference: Access Control: Object Hierarchy and USAGE Privilege

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator applies a Dynamic Data Masking policy that replaces SSN values with 'XXX-XX-XXXX' for all roles except ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS. The policy is applied to the SSN column in the EMPLOYEES table. A user with both ANALYST_ROLE and ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS roles granted queries the table using the ANALYST_ROLE as their active primary role (USE ROLE ANALYST_ROLE). What does the user see for the SSN column?

options.text: The actual SSN value, because the user has ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS granted to them even if it is not their active role
The masked value 'XXX-XX-XXXX', because the masking policy evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() which is ANALYST_ROLE
The actual SSN value, because Snowflake evaluates all granted roles when applying masking
NULL, because the masking policy cannot determine which role to apply

questions.explanation: Dynamic Data Masking policies typically use CURRENT_ROLE() (or CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS') to determine what to show. CURRENT_ROLE() returns the currently active primary role, which is ANALYST_ROLE in this scenario. Even though the user has ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS granted, it is not the active role, so the masking condition is not met and the masked value is returned. To see unmasked data, the user would need to USE ROLE ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS or use secondary roles if the policy checks for secondary roles. This is a key distinction in how masking policies evaluate role context.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to audit all privileges granted to the REPORTING_ROLE. Select TWO commands that provide different aspects of this audit.

options.text: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE REPORTING_ROLE;
SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE REPORTING_ROLE;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES WHERE GRANTEE_NAME = 'REPORTING_ROLE';
DESCRIBE ROLE REPORTING_ROLE PRIVILEGES;

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE REPORTING_ROLE lists all privileges (on databases, schemas, tables, warehouses, etc.) granted TO that role. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE REPORTING_ROLE shows which users and roles have been granted this role (i.e., who has REPORTING_ROLE in their hierarchy). Together they provide a complete picture of what REPORTING_ROLE can do and who uses it. ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES can also provide this information with historical context but has a 45-minute latency. DESCRIBE ROLE PRIVILEGES is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Access Control Auditing: SHOW GRANTS TO/OF ROLE

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is troubleshooting a future grant issue. The command GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS TO ROLE BI_ROLE was run 2 months ago. A new table ANALYTICS.NEW_METRICS was created last week. The BI_ROLE cannot SELECT from NEW_METRICS. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Future grants only apply to tables created within 24 hours of the GRANT command
The table was created by a role different from the one that owns the schema, and future grants do not apply in this case
The future grant was revoked or a REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON FUTURE TABLES was issued, removing the future grant
Future grants require re-issuance every 30 days to remain active

questions.explanation: Future grants persist indefinitely — they do not expire after 24 hours or 30 days. They also apply regardless of which role creates the table within that schema. The most common reason a future grant stops working is that it was explicitly revoked with REVOKE SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS FROM ROLE BI_ROLE, or a blanket REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON FUTURE TABLES was issued. Administrators should run SHOW FUTURE GRANTS IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS to verify the future grant still exists. Option B is incorrect — future grants apply to all new tables in the schema regardless of creator role.

questions.stem: A user activates secondary roles by running USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL. The user's primary role is DEVELOPER_ROLE. The user also has ANALYST_ROLE granted. ANALYST_ROLE has SELECT on a sensitive table. DEVELOPER_ROLE does not. Which statement about this query scenario is correct?

options.text: The query will fail because primary role DEVELOPER_ROLE does not have SELECT on the table
The query will succeed because USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all granted roles simultaneously, and ANALYST_ROLE's SELECT is usable
The query will succeed only if DEVELOPER_ROLE is a parent role of ANALYST_ROLE in the hierarchy
USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL only applies to DDL operations, not DML like SELECT

questions.explanation: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL is a Snowflake feature that activates all roles granted to the user simultaneously during the session. With this enabled, the user can use privileges from any of their granted roles, not just the active primary role. In this scenario, ANALYST_ROLE's SELECT privilege on the sensitive table becomes available even though DEVELOPER_ROLE (the primary role) does not have it. This is a significant access control consideration — administrators should be aware that users with secondary roles active can access anything any of their roles can access. Options A and C are incorrect given secondary roles. Option D is incorrect; secondary roles apply to all SQL operations.
questions.reference: Secondary Roles: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL

questions.stem: An administrator creates a network policy and wants to apply it to the entire Snowflake account. Which command correctly applies the policy at the account level?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = my_corp_policy;
GRANT NETWORK_POLICY my_corp_policy TO ACCOUNT;
CREATE NETWORK POLICY my_corp_policy ACCOUNT_LEVEL = TRUE;
ALTER NETWORK POLICY my_corp_policy SET SCOPE = ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation: The correct command to apply a network policy at the account level is ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. This restricts all access to the account based on the policy's IP rules. The other options use invalid syntax — GRANT NETWORK_POLICY, ACCOUNT_LEVEL = TRUE in CREATE, and ALTER NETWORK POLICY SET SCOPE are not valid Snowflake commands.

questions.stem: A company has implemented network rules using the newer network rule construct. They want to allow access only from specific AWS VPC endpoints. Which type of network rule should be used?

options.text: TYPE = IPV4 with the VPC CIDR range
TYPE = AWSVPCEID with the VPC endpoint IDs
TYPE = HOST with the VPC DNS hostname
TYPE = PRIVATELINK with the endpoint service name

questions.explanation: Network rules support different types for different use cases. TYPE = AWSVPCEID allows specifying AWS VPC endpoint IDs to restrict access to traffic coming from specific VPC endpoints. This is used when you want to ensure traffic originates only from approved VPCs via PrivateLink endpoints. TYPE = IPV4 uses IP addresses/CIDR ranges (public IPs, not VPC endpoints). TYPE = HOST uses DNS hostnames for external functions/API integrations. TYPE = PRIVATELINK is not a valid network rule type.

questions.stem: A security team requires MFA for all interactive users but wants service accounts to bypass MFA for automation. Select TWO approaches to implement this in Snowflake.

options.text: Set up MFA enrollment for all users, then configure service account users to use key pair authentication instead of password-based login (which bypasses MFA)
Create a login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = REQUIRED and apply it at the account level, then create a separate login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = OPTIONAL and assign it to service account users
Enable MFA at the account level and then run ALTER USER svc_account DISABLE MFA = TRUE for each service account
Use a network policy to restrict service accounts to specific IP ranges, then exempt those IPs from MFA requirements

questions.explanation: Key pair authentication for service accounts (option A) is the standard approach — service accounts authenticate via RSA keys and do not use the password/MFA flow at all. MFA only applies to the username/password authentication pathway. Option B using login policies with MFA_ENROLLMENT is also valid — account-level login policy requires MFA, and user-level overrides (OPTIONAL or OPTIONAL policy) exempt specific service account users. ALTER USER DISABLE MFA = TRUE (option C) is not valid Snowflake syntax. Network policies control IP-based access, not MFA requirements (option D).

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is reviewing a security concern: a user's RSA_PUBLIC_KEY was compromised. The user currently has both RSA_PUBLIC_KEY (compromised) and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 (valid and in use by the application) set. What is the immediate action to revoke the compromised key without disrupting the running application?

options.text: ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY;
ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2;
ALTER USER affected_user SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = NULL;
DROP USER affected_user; CREATE USER affected_user RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = '<valid_key>';

questions.explanation: Since RSA_PUBLIC_KEY is the compromised key and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 is the valid key in use by the application, the correct action is to unset only RSA_PUBLIC_KEY. ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY removes only the first key slot, leaving RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 intact and the application unaffected. Option B (UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2) would remove the valid key, disrupting the application. Option C (SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = NULL) may not be valid syntax for key removal. Option D destroys the user account and all associated grants.
questions.reference: Key Pair Authentication: Revoking a Compromised Key

questions.stem: A warehouse is configured with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4, with SCALING_POLICY = STANDARD. When does Snowflake add additional clusters?

options.text: When a single query exceeds a configurable execution time threshold
When queries are queuing and the system determines additional clusters would reduce wait times
At a fixed schedule defined by the administrator
Only when manually triggered using ALTER WAREHOUSE RESUME CLUSTER

questions.explanation: In a multi-cluster warehouse, Snowflake's auto-scaling adds additional clusters when it detects query queuing — queries waiting for an available slot. With SCALING_POLICY = STANDARD, Snowflake scales out promptly when queuing is detected. SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY waits longer before adding clusters (more cost-efficient but slower to scale). Adding clusters is not triggered by individual query execution time, is not scheduled, and does not require manual intervention when auto-scaling is configured.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to minimize idle warehouse costs for a warehouse used only during business hours (8 AM to 6 PM). The warehouse should not be running overnight. Which configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE; schedule a task to resume at 8 AM and suspend at 6 PM
Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE; rely on auto-suspend to handle overnight shutdown automatically
Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 28800 (8 hours) to ensure the warehouse stays running all day
Set INITIALLY_SUSPENDED = TRUE and manually start the warehouse each morning

questions.explanation: With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 seconds and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE, the warehouse automatically suspends 60 seconds after the last query completes (which would be shortly after the last business-hours query), and automatically resumes when users submit queries the next morning. This is the simplest and most cost-effective configuration for business-hours usage. Option A adds unnecessary operational complexity with scheduling tasks. AUTO_SUSPEND = 28800 (8 hours) means the warehouse would stay running for 8 hours after the last query — potentially running all night and wasting credits. INITIALLY_SUSPENDED with manual start (option D) is operationally burdensome.
questions.reference: Warehouse Configuration: AUTO_SUSPEND for Cost Optimization

questions.stem: A large table is showing degraded query performance after months of continuous INSERT operations. Clustering depth has increased significantly. Select TWO actions that can help restore clustering efficiency.

options.text: Enable automatic clustering with ALTER TABLE orders CLUSTER BY (order_date, region);
Run ALTER TABLE orders RECLUSTER to manually trigger a one-time reclustering operation
Increase the warehouse size to compensate for the poor clustering
Drop and recreate the table with data loaded in sorted order by the clustering key

questions.explanation: Enabling automatic clustering (ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY) tells Snowflake to continuously maintain clustering in the background — the service reclusters micro-partitions as DML operations degrade the clustering. Dropping and recreating the table with data loaded in the correct sort order achieves a perfectly clustered initial state (though this requires downtime and a full reload). ALTER TABLE orders RECLUSTER (option B) is not valid Snowflake syntax — Snowflake does not have a manual RECLUSTER command. When automatic clustering is enabled, Snowflake manages reclustering automatically. Increasing warehouse size (option C) may speed up queries marginally but does not fix the clustering problem.

questions.stem: A table has TIME_TRAVEL_RETENTION = 30 days. A user accidentally drops the table. After 25 days, the administrator discovers the dropped table. Can the table be recovered, and how?

options.text: Yes, using UNDROP TABLE table_name; because the table is within the 30-day retention period
No, dropped tables cannot be recovered using Time Travel; only DML changes can be rolled back
Yes, using CREATE TABLE table_name CLONE table_name AT (OFFSET => -2160000);
No, because Time Travel only applies to table data modifications, not DROP TABLE operations

questions.explanation: UNDROP TABLE is the correct command to recover a dropped table within its Time Travel retention period. When a table is dropped, Snowflake retains it in a 'dropped' state for the duration of DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. Since 25 days is within the 30-day retention, UNDROP TABLE table_name will restore it. Time Travel applies to DROP operations as well as DML changes — options B and D are incorrect. Option C (cloning with a time offset) is a different technique for recovering historical data states, not for recovering a dropped table.

questions.stem: A compliance officer needs to verify that all login attempts (both successful and failed) from external applications have been logged for the past 30 days. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and key columns should the administrator reference?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, START_TIME, EXECUTION_STATUS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, EVENT_TIMESTAMP, IS_SUCCESS, ERROR_MESSAGE, CLIENT_TYPE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, QUERY_START_TIME, OBJECTS_ACCESSED
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — columns: USER_NAME, CREATED_ON, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID

questions.explanation: LOGIN_HISTORY is the correct view for auditing authentication events. Key columns include USER_NAME (who attempted), EVENT_TIMESTAMP (when), IS_SUCCESS (whether login succeeded), ERROR_MESSAGE (failure reason for failed attempts), and CLIENT_TYPE/CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID (what application was used). This provides the complete login audit trail for both successes and failures. QUERY_HISTORY covers executed queries, not login events. ACCESS_HISTORY covers data object access, not login events. SESSIONS tracks active session information but not login attempt history.

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to track data lineage — specifically which downstream queries and users accessed data that originated from a source table PROD.SALES.TRANSACTIONS. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view supports column-level lineage tracking?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY joined with EXECUTION_PLAN
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY with DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED and BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED columns
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES filtered by POLICY_KIND = 'LINEAGE'

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY provides the most detailed lineage information in Snowflake. DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED shows the immediate objects a query accessed, while BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED shows the underlying base tables (even when queries went through views or other intermediate objects). Combined, these columns enable column-level data lineage tracking — you can trace which users accessed columns from a source table, even indirectly through views. QUERY_HISTORY contains query text but not structured lineage. OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES tracks static schema-level dependencies (view → table) but not runtime access lineage. POLICY_REFERENCES tracks policy assignments, not lineage.
questions.reference: Account Usage: ACCESS_HISTORY for Data Lineage

options.text: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask; (Directly replaces the existing policy)
ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn UNSET MASKING POLICY; then ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask;
DROP MASKING POLICY ssn_mask; then ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask;
ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn REPLACE MASKING POLICY ssn_mask WITH new_ssn_mask;

questions.explanation: In Snowflake, you can directly SET a new masking policy on a column that already has one — the new SET replaces the existing policy assignment in a single atomic operation. This is the simplest approach. Option B (UNSET then SET) also works but requires two commands. Option C (dropping the old policy) would fail if the policy is still applied to any column — you would need to unset it first. Option D uses an invalid REPLACE syntax that does not exist in Snowflake.
questions.reference: Dynamic Data Masking: Replacing Applied Policies

questions.stem: An administrator creates a resource monitor: CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 FREQUENCY = MONTHLY START_TIMESTAMP = IMMEDIATELY TRIGGERS ON 90 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY; The monitor is assigned to PROD_WH. Three days before month end, the warehouse hits 100% and is suspended. The business needs the warehouse running urgently. What must the administrator do?

options.text: Wait until the start of next month when the monthly quota resets automatically
Increase the CREDIT_QUOTA on the resource monitor, then resume the warehouse
Run ALTER WAREHOUSE PROD_WH RESUME — the warehouse can be manually resumed even when suspended by a resource monitor
Delete the resource monitor assignment, resume the warehouse, and reassign the monitor next month

questions.explanation: When a warehouse is suspended by a resource monitor, it cannot be simply resumed with ALTER WAREHOUSE RESUME — the resource monitor will immediately re-suspend it because the quota is still exceeded. The correct approach is to first increase the CREDIT_QUOTA on the resource monitor (ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 1200, for example), and then resume the warehouse. Option C is partially true in that ALTER WAREHOUSE RESUME will briefly resume it, but the monitor will immediately re-suspend it. Option A requires waiting until month end. Option D works operationally but is overly disruptive and removes monitoring entirely.

questions.stem: An administrator sets ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 7200. A concurrent DML operation on table ORDERS holds a lock. A second transaction waits for the lock. What happens after 7200 seconds if the lock has not been released?

options.text: The second transaction is automatically cancelled with a lock timeout error
The first transaction (lock holder) is forcibly rolled back to free the lock
Both transactions are suspended until an administrator manually resolves the conflict
The second transaction continues waiting indefinitely because LOCK_TIMEOUT does not apply to DML locks

questions.explanation: LOCK_TIMEOUT specifies how long a statement will wait to acquire a lock. After the timeout period (7200 seconds in this case), the waiting statement is cancelled and returns an error — it does not force the lock-holding transaction to roll back. The lock holder continues unaffected. The cancelled statement can be retried. This parameter prevents indefinite blocking but does not penalize the lock holder.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to check the effective value of the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter for a specific warehouse and understand whether it was set at the account, warehouse, or session level. Which command provides this level detail?

options.text: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN WAREHOUSE my_wh;
DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE my_wh PARAMETERS;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_PARAMETERS WHERE WAREHOUSE_NAME = 'MY_WH';
SHOW WAREHOUSES LIKE 'MY_WH' PARAMETERS;

questions.explanation: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'pattern' IN WAREHOUSE warehouse_name shows the current value of parameters set at the warehouse level, including whether the value was explicitly set at the warehouse level or inherited from the account level. The output includes a 'level' column (account, warehouse, session) indicating where the effective value was set. DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE with PARAMETERS is not valid Snowflake syntax. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have a WAREHOUSE_PARAMETERS view. SHOW WAREHOUSES does not show parameter details.
questions.reference: Account Administration: SHOW PARAMETERS IN WAREHOUSE

questions.stem: A user contacts support because they are unable to log into Snowflake and receive the message 'User is locked'. The administrator confirms the user hit the maximum failed login attempts. Which command immediately restores login access for this user?

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET DISABLED = FALSE;
ALTER USER locked_user UNLOCK;
ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
GRANT LOGIN TO USER locked_user;

questions.explanation: ALTER USER ... SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately clears the lockout by reducing the remaining lock duration to zero. This is the correct Snowflake command for unlocking a user account after failed login lockout. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a user that was explicitly disabled by an administrator — not the same as clearing a login lockout. ALTER USER UNLOCK and GRANT LOGIN are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A Snowflake user logs in but complains they have to manually run USE ROLE and USE WAREHOUSE every session. The administrator wants to configure the user to automatically use REPORTING_ROLE and REPORTING_WH. Which ALTER USER parameters should be set?

options.text: ALTER USER user1 SET DEFAULT_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
ALTER USER user1 SET AUTO_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' AUTO_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
ALTER USER user1 SET SESSION_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' SESSION_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
ALTER USER user1 SET LOGIN_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' LOGIN_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';

questions.explanation: DEFAULT_ROLE and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE are the valid Snowflake user properties that automatically set the role and warehouse at session start. When the user logs in, Snowflake automatically activates the DEFAULT_ROLE (if it is granted to the user) and sets the DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE without requiring manual USE commands. AUTO_ROLE, SESSION_ROLE, AUTO_WAREHOUSE, SESSION_WAREHOUSE, LOGIN_ROLE, and LOGIN_WAREHOUSE are not valid Snowflake user properties.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to add governance documentation to all custom roles, noting the team that owns each role. The FINANCE_TEAM_ROLE needs the comment 'Owned by Finance Operations team'. Which command is correct?

options.text: COMMENT ON ROLE finance_team_role IS 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
ALTER ROLE finance_team_role SET COMMENT = 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
UPDATE ROLE finance_team_role SET DESCRIPTION = 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
ALTER ROLE finance_team_role MODIFY COMMENT 'Owned by Finance Operations team';

questions.explanation: The correct Snowflake DDL to add or update the comment on a role is ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = 'text'. This comment is visible in SHOW ROLES and in the Snowflake web UI, supporting governance and role documentation practices. COMMENT ON ROLE ... IS is a SQL standard syntax not used in Snowflake for role objects. UPDATE ROLE and ALTER ROLE MODIFY COMMENT are not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Account Administration: Role Comments for Governance

questions.stem: A Snowflake task is calling a stored procedure. Inside the stored procedure, the code needs to conditionally execute different logic depending on which task triggered it. Which function allows the procedure to identify its calling task?

options.text: CURRENT_STATEMENT()
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
INVOKER_TASK_NAME()
SYSTEM$GET_TASK_CONTEXT('task_name')

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() returns the fully-qualified name of the task that is currently executing the procedure. This enables branching logic inside shared stored procedures that are called by multiple different tasks. CURRENT_STATEMENT() returns the SQL text of the currently executing statement. INVOKER_TASK_NAME() and SYSTEM$GET_TASK_CONTEXT() are not valid Snowflake functions.

questions.stem: An infrastructure-as-code pipeline creates roles as part of environment setup. The pipeline runs on every deployment. Which role creation pattern prevents errors when the role already exists from a previous deployment?

options.text: CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE env_role;
CREATE ROLE env_role IF NOT FOUND;
CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS env_role;
UPSERT ROLE env_role;

questions.explanation: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is the idempotent form that creates the role only if it does not already exist. If the role exists, the statement succeeds without error and without modifying the existing role or its grants. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE (option A) would drop and recreate the role, destroying all existing grants — dangerous in a re-run pipeline. CREATE ROLE IF NOT FOUND and UPSERT ROLE are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to quickly find all roles that include 'ENGINEER' in their name. Which Snowflake command accomplishes this?

options.text: SELECT ROLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME ILIKE '%ENGINEER%';
SHOW ROLES LIKE '%ENGINEER%';
DESCRIBE ROLES CONTAINING 'ENGINEER';
LIST ROLES FILTER NAME = '*ENGINEER*';

questions.explanation: SHOW ROLES LIKE '%ENGINEER%' uses the standard SQL LIKE wildcard pattern to filter roles whose names contain 'ENGINEER' anywhere in the name. The % wildcard matches any sequence of characters before and after. Option A (ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES with ILIKE) also works for filtering and supports case-insensitive matching, but SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct DDL approach. DESCRIBE ROLES CONTAINING and LIST ROLES FILTER are not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Account Administration: SHOW ROLES LIKE Pattern

questions.stem: Snowflake is preparing to enable behavior change bundle '2025_01' for all accounts. An administrator wants to test the impact in a sandbox account before it becomes mandatory. Which system function enables the bundle in the sandbox?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT ENABLE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2025_01';
SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2025_01');
SET SNOWFLAKE_BCR_BUNDLE = '2025_01';
CREATE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_TEST FOR BUNDLE '2025_01';

questions.explanation: SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('bundle_name') is the Snowflake system function to opt an account into a specific behavior change bundle before it is automatically enabled. This allows testing of the bundle's impact on existing queries and procedures in a non-production account. ALTER ACCOUNT ENABLE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE (option A) is not valid Snowflake DDL syntax. SET SNOWFLAKE_BCR_BUNDLE and CREATE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_TEST are not valid Snowflake commands.
questions.reference: Account Administration: Testing Behavior Change Bundles

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a resource monitor with NOTIFY_USERS = ('DBA_ADMIN', 'CFO_USER') and a NOTIFY trigger at 80%. The CFO_USER does not have a Snowflake email address configured. What happens when the 80% threshold is crossed?

options.text: The notification fails entirely because one listed user has no email address
Snowflake sends the notification to DBA_ADMIN only; CFO_USER is silently skipped
Snowflake notifies all ACCOUNTADMIN users instead since the NOTIFY_USERS list is invalid
The resource monitor is disabled until all NOTIFY_USERS have valid email addresses

questions.explanation: Resource monitor notifications are sent on a best-effort basis per user. Users in the NOTIFY_USERS list who have a valid email address configured in their Snowflake user profile receive the notification. Users without a configured email address are silently skipped — the notification is not blocked or failed for other recipients. The resource monitor continues functioning normally. Administrators should ensure all NOTIFY_USERS have email addresses set with ALTER USER ... SET EMAIL = 'address'.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: NOTIFY_USERS and Email Requirements

questions.stem: A resource monitor is assigned to a warehouse and the SUSPEND trigger fires at 100% quota. The administrator immediately increases the quota with ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR mon SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 1500. What additional step is required to resume the warehouse?

options.text: No additional step is needed; increasing the quota automatically resumes the warehouse
Run ALTER WAREHOUSE wh RESUME to manually restart the suspended warehouse
Wait for the next monitoring cycle (up to 1 hour) when the warehouse auto-resumes
Detach the resource monitor from the warehouse, resume it, then reattach the monitor

questions.explanation: Increasing the CREDIT_QUOTA on a resource monitor removes the reason for the suspension, but it does not automatically resume the warehouse. The administrator must explicitly run ALTER WAREHOUSE wh RESUME (or the Snowflake UI equivalent) to restart the warehouse. Once resumed, the warehouse operates normally with the new higher quota. Increasing the quota does not trigger an automatic resume. Waiting for an auto-resume cycle does not apply — suspended warehouses do not have a periodic auto-resume. Detaching and reattaching the monitor (option D) would also work but is unnecessary if the quota increase is sufficient.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: Manual Resume After Quota Increase

questions.stem: An administrator wants to understand query queuing pressure on ANALYTICS_WH over the past week. Select TWO data sources that provide this insight.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING — provides 5-minute interval snapshots of queued, running, and blocked query counts
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column shows how long individual queries waited due to warehouse overload
SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS — the QUEUE_DEPTH column shows historical queue depth per warehouse
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — the QUEUED_QUERIES column shows total queuing events

questions.explanation: WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides aggregate warehouse-level snapshots every 5 minutes showing the count of running, queued, and blocked queries — ideal for identifying queuing patterns and peak periods. QUERY_HISTORY has a QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column that shows, for each individual query, how long it waited due to warehouse overload. Together these views provide both aggregate and per-query queuing visibility. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS does not have a QUEUE_DEPTH column. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption, not query queue metrics.

questions.stem: A company's storage costs have unexpectedly tripled over three months. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides a time-series history of daily storage usage that can be charted to identify the trend?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides daily snapshots of total storage consumed by the account, including AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES, AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES, and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES. With 1 year of retention, it can be queried to chart storage growth trends over time. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS is a point-in-time view of current storage per table, not a historical series. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS also shows current state, not historical trends. DATABASE_REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY tracks replication egress, not overall storage.
questions.reference: Resource Monitoring: STORAGE_USAGE for Trend Analysis

questions.stem: An administrator notices high serverless credit charges and suspects serverless tasks (not Snowpipe). Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides credit consumption details per serverless task execution?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'SERVERLESS_TASK'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation: SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY is the dedicated ACCOUNT_USAGE view for serverless task credit consumption. It shows per-execution records including the task name, database, schema, start/end times, and credits consumed. This enables identifying which specific serverless tasks are driving credit usage. TASK_HISTORY exists for all tasks but focuses on execution state and errors rather than credit consumption. While METERING_DAILY_HISTORY does show aggregate serverless task credits by day, it does not break down by individual task. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY is for Snowpipe, not tasks.

questions.stem: A team wants to track Snowpipe credit consumption by individual pipe to identify which data ingestion pipeline is most expensive. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this per-pipe breakdown?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE WAREHOUSE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE'

questions.explanation: PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-pipe credit consumption records for Snowpipe. Each row includes the pipe name, database, schema, credits used, bytes inserted, and the time period — enabling cost attribution to specific ingestion pipelines. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows aggregate Snowpipe credits but does not break down by individual pipe. COPY_HISTORY shows file load history but not credit attribution. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY does not have a SNOWPIPE warehouse type — Snowpipe runs on serverless compute, not virtual warehouses.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to give REPORTING_ROLE access to all tables currently in the ANALYTICS schema AND ensure access to tables created in the future. Select TWO commands that together accomplish this.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL CURRENT AND FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
ALTER SCHEMA analytics SET AUTO_GRANT SELECT TO ROLE reporting_role;

questions.explanation: Two separate GRANT commands are needed: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES covers existing tables at the time of execution, and GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES covers all tables created after the grant is issued. There is no combined ALL CURRENT AND FUTURE syntax (option C is invalid). ALTER SCHEMA SET AUTO_GRANT (option D) is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Access Control: Bulk and Future Grants

questions.stem: A role ANALYST_ROLE was accidentally granted MODIFY on table SALES.ORDERS. The administrator needs to remove only the MODIFY privilege without affecting the role's SELECT privilege on the same table. Which command achieves this?

options.text: REVOKE ALL ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role;
REVOKE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role;
ALTER ROLE analyst_role REMOVE PRIVILEGE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE sales.orders TO ROLE sysadmin REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;

questions.explanation: REVOKE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role removes exactly the MODIFY privilege from the role on that specific table. The role's SELECT privilege (and any other privileges) on that table remain intact. REVOKE ALL removes all privileges, not just MODIFY. ALTER ROLE REMOVE PRIVILEGE is not valid Snowflake syntax. GRANT OWNERSHIP REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership and removes all grants — far more disruptive than required.

questions.stem: A stored procedure runs with EXECUTE AS OWNER privileges. The procedure calls IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('ADMIN_ROLE') to check if the calling user has admin privileges before executing a sensitive operation. The calling user has ADMIN_ROLE in their role hierarchy but is currently using DATA_ROLE as the active role. What does IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE return?

options.text: TRUE, because ADMIN_ROLE is granted to the invoking user even though it is not their active role
FALSE, because IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE only checks the currently active primary role, not the full hierarchy
TRUE, because the function checks whether the invoker has ADMIN_ROLE anywhere in their granted role hierarchy
Error — IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE cannot be called from within an EXECUTE AS OWNER procedure

questions.explanation: IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') checks whether the role is anywhere in the invoker's granted role hierarchy — not just the currently active primary role. If the calling user has ADMIN_ROLE granted (directly or transitively), the function returns TRUE. This is intentional behavior: it allows the procedure to check for a broad privilege grant without requiring the user to switch their active role. The function is specifically designed for use in EXECUTE AS OWNER stored procedures to make invoker-context access decisions.

questions.stem: A security auditor notes that a non-admin role has OWNERSHIP on several production tables. Select TWO security implications of this situation.

options.text: The role can GRANT any privilege on those tables to any other role, including to PUBLIC
The role can drop those tables even if it does not have USAGE on the schema
The role can transfer OWNERSHIP to another role, including granting it to ACCOUNTADMIN
The role automatically inherits ACCOUNTADMIN privileges because it owns production tables

questions.explanation: OWNERSHIP privilege grants full control over the object, including the ability to: (A) GRANT any privilege on the owned tables to any role (including PUBLIC, creating a data exposure risk) and (C) transfer OWNERSHIP to another role. Ownership transfer is done via GRANT OWNERSHIP, and the current owner can choose which role receives the object. Option B is incorrect — USAGE on the schema is still required to access or drop objects within it. Option D is incorrect — owning objects does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN or any account-level privileges.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is consuming data from a data share provided by a vendor. The administrator needs to grant the DATA_TEAM_ROLE access to query tables in the shared database VENDOR_DB. Which privilege grant is specific to shared databases?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;

questions.explanation: For databases created from inbound data shares, the privilege IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE is the correct grant. This single privilege grants the role access to all objects in the shared database as defined by the share provider. Standard USAGE grants on shared databases do not work the same way as local databases. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES is valid for local databases but cannot be applied to shared databases directly. REFERENCE_USAGE is for cross-database views and is a different concept.
questions.reference: Data Sharing: Consumer Account Privilege Grant

questions.stem: An administrator needs to view all objects (tables, schemas, warehouses) that REPORTING_ROLE currently has privileges on, and who originally granted those privileges. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE reporting_role;
SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE reporting_role;
DESCRIBE ROLE reporting_role;
SHOW ROLE reporting_role GRANTS;

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE role_name lists all privileges granted to that specific role, including the privilege type, the object type, the object name, and the GRANTED_BY column showing who issued each grant. This is the command for viewing what a role can do. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE (option B) shows who has been granted this role (users and roles that hold it), not what privileges it has. DESCRIBE ROLE shows basic role metadata but not its grants. SHOW ROLE GRANTS is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Access Control: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE

questions.stem: A company deploys a Snowflake Native App. The app defines two Application Roles: APP_VIEWER and APP_ADMIN. A consumer account administrator wants to give their ANALYST_ROLE users access to the app's viewer functionality. What is the correct step?

options.text: GRANT APPLICATION ROLE myapp.app_viewer TO ROLE analyst_role;
GRANT USAGE ON APPLICATION myapp TO ROLE analyst_role;
ALTER APPLICATION myapp GRANT VIEWER TO ROLE analyst_role;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN APPLICATION myapp TO ROLE analyst_role;

questions.explanation: To grant a Snowflake Native App's Application Role to an account role, the syntax is GRANT APPLICATION ROLE app_name.role_name TO ROLE account_role. In this case, GRANT APPLICATION ROLE myapp.app_viewer TO ROLE analyst_role assigns the app's viewer Application Role to the consumer's ANALYST_ROLE. GRANT USAGE ON APPLICATION (option B) does not grant the specific Application Role. ALTER APPLICATION GRANT VIEWER and GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN APPLICATION are not valid Snowflake syntax for Native Apps.
questions.reference: Native Apps: Granting Application Roles to Account Roles

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator has applied an account-level network policy. Later, a specific service account SVC_INTEGRATION needs to connect from an IP not in the account policy. The administrator creates a new policy SVC_POLICY that includes the service account's IP. Which command applies this policy specifically to the service account?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_integration SET NETWORK_POLICY = svc_policy;
ALTER ACCOUNT OVERRIDE NETWORK_POLICY = svc_policy FOR USER svc_integration;
GRANT NETWORK POLICY svc_policy TO USER svc_integration;
CREATE NETWORK POLICY EXCEPTION FOR USER svc_integration USING svc_policy;

questions.explanation: Network policies are applied to individual users using ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy entirely for that user. This is the Snowflake mechanism for per-user network policy exceptions. ALTER ACCOUNT OVERRIDE, GRANT NETWORK POLICY, and CREATE NETWORK POLICY EXCEPTION are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to find all network policies defined in the Snowflake account and see their allowed and blocked IP lists. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES;
LIST NETWORK POLICIES;
DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICIES ALL;

questions.explanation: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all network policies in the account with their name, comment, entries count for allowed and blocked lists, and which objects (account or users) they are assigned to. The full IP lists can be viewed with DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY policy_name. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES system view. LIST NETWORK POLICIES and DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICIES ALL are not valid Snowflake commands.

questions.stem: A Snowflake external stage pointing to an Azure Blob container is failing with an access error. An administrator suspects a misconfigured storage integration. Which Snowflake function can verify whether the stage can successfully connect to the external location?

options.text: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@my_azure_stage')
SYSTEM$CHECK_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('my_azure_integration')
SYSTEM$TEST_STAGE_CONNECTIVITY('@my_azure_stage')
SELECT STAGE_STATUS FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES WHERE STAGE_NAME = 'MY_AZURE_STAGE';

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@stage_name') validates the connection to an external stage by testing whether Snowflake can authenticate and access the external cloud storage location. It returns diagnostic information about the connectivity and permission state. SYSTEM$CHECK_STORAGE_INTEGRATION and SYSTEM$TEST_STAGE_CONNECTIVITY are not valid Snowflake system functions. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES does not have a real-time STAGE_STATUS connectivity column.

questions.stem: An organization uses Snowflake on AWS with PrivateLink enabled. They have a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('203.0.113.0/24'). A client connecting through PrivateLink from a VPC with private IP 10.0.1.5 (not in the allowed list) attempts to connect. What occurs?

options.text: The connection is blocked because 10.0.1.5 is not in the ALLOWED_IP_LIST
The connection succeeds because PrivateLink traffic bypasses IP-based network policy enforcement
The connection is blocked because PrivateLink requires a separate AWSVPCEID network rule, not an IP policy
The connection is blocked and an alert is sent to the NOTIFY_USERS list

questions.explanation: When clients connect through AWS PrivateLink, their traffic arrives at Snowflake without presenting a public IP address in the way that IP-based network policies evaluate. As a result, standard IP-based network policies are bypassed for PrivateLink connections. To enforce network-level restrictions for PrivateLink traffic, administrators should use AWSVPCEID-type network rules to restrict by VPC endpoint ID. This is a commonly misunderstood interaction between PrivateLink and network policies.
questions.reference: Network Security: PrivateLink Bypasses IP Network Policies

questions.stem: A security team wants to restrict Snowflake access to only connections originating from a specific set of approved AWS VPC endpoints (vpce-0a1b2c3d, vpce-0e5f6789). Which is the correct CREATE NETWORK RULE command for this?

options.text: CREATE NETWORK RULE vpc_rule TYPE = AWSVPCEID VALUE_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789') MODE = INGRESS;
CREATE NETWORK POLICY vpc_policy ALLOWED_VPC_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');
CREATE NETWORK RULE vpc_rule TYPE = IPV4 VALUE_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');
ALTER ACCOUNT SET VPC_ALLOWED_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');

questions.explanation: The correct syntax to create a network rule for VPC endpoint-based access control uses TYPE = AWSVPCEID and lists the VPC endpoint IDs in VALUE_LIST. The MODE = INGRESS specifies this is for inbound connection control. This network rule is then included in a network policy for enforcement. ALLOWED_VPC_LIST is not a valid network policy parameter (option B). TYPE = IPV4 with VPC endpoint IDs (option C) is incorrect — VPC endpoint IDs are not IP addresses. ALTER ACCOUNT SET VPC_ALLOWED_LIST (option D) is not a valid Snowflake parameter.

questions.stem: An administrator notices that a staging table used for ETL has TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 45GB but the table only holds 2GB of active data. The staging table is overwritten every night. What is the most effective way to eliminate this wasted Time Travel storage?

options.text: Reduce DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS at the account level to 0
Run TRUNCATE TABLE staging_table to clear the Time Travel history
ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
Enable automatic clustering on the staging table to compact and merge old micro-partitions

questions.explanation: For a staging table that is overwritten nightly and where Time Travel is not needed, setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 at the table level disables Time Travel for that specific table, eliminating the TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES storage cost. This is the precise, targeted fix. Reducing retention at the account level (option A) would affect all tables account-wide, violating least-impact principle. TRUNCATE TABLE clears current data but does not immediately eliminate all Time Travel history. Automatic clustering (option D) reorganizes micro-partitions for query performance — it does not reduce Time Travel storage.
questions.reference: Storage & Compute: Reducing Time Travel Storage with DATA_RETENTION = 0

questions.stem: A developer creates both a TEMPORARY table and a TRANSIENT table with the same schema in a Snowflake session. What happens to the TEMPORARY table when the session ends?

options.text: The temporary table is automatically dropped at session end; the transient table persists
Both tables are dropped when the session ends because both types are session-scoped
Both tables persist across sessions — the difference is only in their Fail-Safe behavior
The temporary table persists for 24 hours after session end before automatic deletion

questions.explanation: Temporary tables are strictly session-scoped — they are automatically dropped when the session that created them ends, and are not visible to other sessions. Transient tables, like permanent tables, persist across sessions and are visible account-wide. The key differences between transient and permanent tables are: transient tables have no Fail-Safe period and support only 0 or 1 day of Time Travel (vs up to 90 days for Enterprise edition permanent tables). Temporary tables also have no Fail-Safe and limited Time Travel, but are additionally session-scoped.
questions.reference: Table Types: Temporary vs Transient Session Scope

questions.stem: An administrator wants to verify the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS setting for all tables in a specific schema to identify tables with non-standard retention values. Which approach correctly retrieves this information?

options.text: SHOW TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema; — check the retention_time column
DESCRIBE SCHEMA mydb.myschema TABLES;
SELECT TABLE_NAME, DATA_RETENTION_DAYS FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';
SHOW DATABASES LIKE 'MYDB' TABLES;

questions.explanation: SHOW TABLES IN SCHEMA schema_name outputs a row per table with a retention_time column that shows the effective DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS setting for each table. This is the quickest way to review retention settings across a schema. DESCRIBE SCHEMA with TABLES is not valid syntax. ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLES does exist and has a RETENTION_TIME column, making option C conceptually valid but the column name is RETENTION_TIME, not DATA_RETENTION_DAYS. SHOW DATABASES LIKE with TABLES is not valid syntax.
questions.reference: Storage & Compute: SHOW TABLES Retention Column

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is currently of type STANDARD. A Snowpark team needs a warehouse with high memory for ML training. Which command changes the warehouse type to SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
DROP WAREHOUSE ml_wh; CREATE WAREHOUSE ml_wh TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh SET MEMORY_OPTIMIZED = TRUE;
ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh MODIFY TYPE = 'SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED';

questions.explanation: ALTER WAREHOUSE warehouse_name SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED is the correct syntax to change an existing warehouse to the Snowpark-optimized type. This command can be applied to a running or suspended warehouse. The warehouse type change takes effect on the next warehouse start. MEMORY_OPTIMIZED = TRUE (option C) is not a valid warehouse parameter. ALTER WAREHOUSE MODIFY TYPE (option D) is not valid Snowflake syntax. While dropping and recreating (option B) also works, it is unnecessary when ALTER WAREHOUSE supports the type change directly.
questions.reference: Storage & Compute: ALTER WAREHOUSE SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE

questions.stem: An administrator queries TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and finds TABLE_A has ACTIVE_BYTES = 10GB, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 50GB, FAILSAFE_BYTES = 40GB. Select TWO accurate statements about this storage profile.

options.text: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on TABLE_A immediately drops the 50GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES
The 40GB of FAILSAFE_BYTES are billable to the customer but are only recoverable by Snowflake Support
The high TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES relative to ACTIVE_BYTES suggests the table has a high rate of DML changes (updates/deletes) creating many historical versions
Cloning TABLE_A to create TABLE_A_CLONE will double the ACTIVE_BYTES cost immediately

questions.explanation: Fail-Safe storage (FAILSAFE_BYTES) is billed to the customer at the same rate as active storage, but it is only accessible by Snowflake Support — customers cannot query or restore Fail-Safe data directly (option B is true). A high ratio of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to ACTIVE_BYTES typically indicates a high volume of DML operations (updates, deletes, inserts) that create many micro-partition versions in Time Travel history (option C is true). Setting retention to 0 does not immediately purge Time Travel data — it ages out over time via background processes (option A is false). Zero-copy cloning does not double ACTIVE_BYTES immediately — only divergent data creates additional storage (option D is false).

questions.stem: A security team needs to audit all data that was transferred out of the Snowflake account to external locations (external stages, S3 buckets, etc.) over the past 60 days. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this egress data transfer history?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TYPE = 'UNLOAD'

questions.explanation: DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY in ACCOUNT_USAGE records all data transfers between Snowflake and external locations, including COPY INTO (external stage) operations. It includes the transfer direction, bytes transferred, source and destination regions, and timestamps — making it the primary view for egress auditing and cross-region data transfer cost analysis. COPY_HISTORY tracks file-level COPY INTO operations but is more focused on load status than egress tracking. STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view. Filtering ACCESS_HISTORY by query type is not the designated approach for egress auditing.
questions.reference: Audit & Compliance: DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY for Egress Monitoring

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to identify all client applications connecting to Snowflake and flag any outdated JDBC driver versions. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and column combination provides this client application tracking?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT columns
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS — CLIENT_VERSION column

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS provides the most comprehensive client application tracking. The CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column contains the application name and version (e.g., 'JDBC 3.13.22'), and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT contains additional metadata about the client platform. By querying SESSIONS, administrators can identify all distinct client applications connecting to Snowflake and filter for outdated driver versions. LOGIN_HISTORY also has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID but captures only login events, not the full session context. QUERY_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID per query but requires deduplication for unique client identification. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS does not have a CLIENT_VERSION column.
questions.reference: Audit & Compliance: SESSIONS for Client Application Tracking

questions.stem: An ORGADMIN user wants to view credit usage across all accounts in the Snowflake organization to identify which accounts are highest consumers. Which schema and view provides this cross-account visibility?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — queried from any account in the org
SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY — accessed by ORGADMIN from the org admin account
SNOWFLAKE.ORG_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — available to all accounts in the organization
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — with a cross-account JOIN using ACCOUNT_LOCATOR

questions.explanation: Organization-level credit usage visibility is available through the ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema, accessible to users with the ORGADMIN role from the organization administrator account. USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY (and related views like METERING_DAILY_HISTORY in ORGANIZATION_USAGE) provide cross-account credit consumption data. ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is scoped to a single account and cannot be queried across accounts. There is no cross-account JOIN mechanism available to regular users. ORG_USAGE is not the correct schema name — it is ORGANIZATION_USAGE.
